78 / 100 Dissertation 2025

Please Marten the Gap

Spatial Prioritisation for Red Squirrel Conservation

PythonGeoPandasArcGIS ProMCDALandscape Ecology

Project Overview

Invasive grey squirrels have displaced native red squirrels from much of the United Kingdom, and culling programmes are increasingly controversial and underfunded. This dissertation develops a spatial prioritisation framework to guide red squirrel conservation as the native pine marten — a predator that selectively suppresses the invasive grey squirrel — recolonises Cumbria after being nearly functionally extinct since the 1700s.

Working with open-source data and libraries, I built a Python-based algorithm that models how pine martens reduce grey squirrel densities and then ranks candidate strongholds using a multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA). Criteria include residual grey squirrel density, core habitat area, and management feasibility derived from landowner willingness proxies. By exploring trade-offs across three suppression scenarios (low, moderate, high), the study provides an evidence-based roadmap for NGOs allocating limited resources.

Motivation

This project was motivated by four core questions:

  1. How will grey squirrel distribution respond to pine marten recovery under different predation efficiencies?
  2. Where should conservation NGOs prioritise strongholds when balancing grey cost, core habitat area, and management feasibility?
  3. How robust are existing policies such as the Lake District Stronghold Complex when predator dynamics change?
  4. How can a transparent modelling framework build trust with stakeholders and adapt to new ecological data?

Methodology

The analysis integrates landscape ecology, geospatial programming, and computational decision-making:

  • Suppression modelling: Baseline grey squirrel densities were reduced using three pine marten suppression coefficients derived from field studies. The residual density surface becomes the grey cost criterion in the MCDA.
  • Habitat and feasibility mapping: High-resolution land cover and woodland data were reclassified to identify suitable habitat patches. Each 100 m grid cell receives a feasibility score from 1 (Low) to 4 (High) based on grant uptake, Countryside Stewardship schemes, and Forestry England ownership.
  • Cluster-based MCDA: A custom greedy algorithm grows clusters of habitat patches from seed sites, scoring each cluster across grey cost, core area, and feasibility. Thousands of candidate strongholds are evaluated per suppression scenario.
  • Correlation analysis: Examined how the influence of each criterion shifts with suppression level — revealing adaptive management insights.

Results

  • Whinlatter Forest is consistently identified as the top stronghold across all scenarios.
  • An expanded Greystoke site ranks second under low suppression; Elterwater emerges as robust across scenarios.
  • Significant suppression shifts priority away from conifer plantations toward broadleaf and mixed woodlands, challenging current policy.
  • Correlation analysis shows grey cost drives scores when grey squirrels are abundant; core habitat area rises in importance as suppression increases.

Implications for NGOs

The framework provides ranked woodland complexes accounting for ecological impact and local feasibility, helping NGOs target limited resources effectively. All code is open-source and replicable, fostering collaboration and trust among stakeholders. The scenario outputs enable phased investment planning aligned with predator recovery timelines.

Final Reflection

The study demonstrates that embracing a returning predator can transform invasive species management. By linking pine marten recovery with a transparent prioritisation tool, it moves beyond theoretical discourse to provide actionable guidance for NGOs — revealing hidden trade-offs between ecology and feasibility, challenging assumptions about conifer-dominated reserves, and proposing adaptive pathways that align with human and ecological realities.

Resources

Skills Demonstrated

  • Geospatial programming (2,000+ line Python workflow)
  • Landscape ecology metrics
  • Multi-criteria decision analysis
  • Feasibility assessment & stakeholder proxies
  • Raster and vector processing
  • Scientific communication
